essay · long read

The agentic dev loop

A bit on how feedback loops shape what AI-assisted coding actually feels like — and why the slowest loop in your day is the one quietly deciding what kind of work you can do at all.

by matthew berryhill
·
posted 2026-04-12
·
updated 2026-04-14
·
topic agentic-dev
table of contents
  1. The loop
  2. Why latency is the feature
  3. What breaks at scale
  4. Where humans stay
  5. A short prediction
# on this page
  1. The loop
  2. Why latency is the feature
  3. What breaks at scale
  4. Where humans stay
  5. A short prediction

The loop

The smallest reliable unit of agentic dev is the loop: write, run, observe, adjust. That sounds like every dev cycle ever written down. The difference is who's doing each step, and how fast the connections between them close.

When the loop is sub-second, you stop thinking about it. The model is a co-typer. When it's thirty seconds, you start opening other tabs. When it's three minutes, the work shape changes entirely — you batch your asks, you guess at outcomes instead of testing them, you stop trying the next thing because you don't want to wait again. Loops are not just a time cost; they're a permission to attempt the next move.

This is the thing most dev tooling discourse misses. We argue about model quality, context windows, autocomplete vs. agent, when the load-bearing variable is just: how long does it take to go around once?[1]

Why latency is the feature

I keep coming back to a small numbers table. Here's the cost of a single round trip, mapped to what the developer ends up doing:

# loop time → felt behavior
< 1s     keystroke-like; no context loss
1–5s    you keep looking at the screen
5–30s   you glance at slack
30–120s you context-switch, sometimes for good
2–10m   you start writing the next prompt
> 10m   you stop testing your assumptions

None of these numbers are surprising on their own. What's surprising is that the loop time is set by the slowest, least observable step. Usually it's not the model — it's the verification: running the test, hitting the page, watching the screenshot render, waiting for a CI lane.

In other words, the latency budget of an agent is set downstream of the model. You can swap to a faster model and not feel a thing, because the tail is in the verifier.

The right question is not "how fast does the model produce a diff?" but "how fast can the developer know whether that diff was right?"a coworker, in a 1:1, half by accident

Once you frame it that way, a lot of tooling decisions invert. Pre-warmed sandboxes start to feel more important than another inference shave. Streamed UI previews matter more than streaming tokens. watch mode beats faster cold starts.

What breaks at scale

The naive scaling story for agentic dev is fan-out — one agent becomes many, many becomes many-many. In practice I keep running into three failure modes that show up the moment a single developer is supervising more than one loop:

  • Attention runs out before tokens do. You can absolutely afford a dozen parallel agents. You cannot afford the cognitive cost of reviewing what twelve agents did in the last ninety seconds. Review bandwidth is the new bottleneck.
  • Loops compound multiplicatively, not additively. Two five-minute loops in series cost ten minutes. Two five-minute loops that depend on each other cost ten minutes plus the cost of holding the intermediate state in your head. The second number is the one nobody budgets for.
  • State sprawl wins quietly. Each loop spawns artifacts: a branch, a temp file, a screenshot, a note, an environment, a process. None of them are expensive individually. After a week the workspace is a library you can't find the index for.

The honest fix for all three is the same: shrink the number of loops you're personally running and make the survivors faster, not the inverse.

note I had to learn this twice. The first time I optimized the wrong end and ended up running four agents on the same problem and reviewing none of them. The second time I went back to one agent with a fifteen-second feedback loop, and shipped more in a Saturday than the four-agent week.

Where humans stay

There's a stack of work an agent can already do better than I can: searching, naming, normalizing, threading state through five files, writing the dull half of a test. I do not miss any of that.

What stays mine, at least for now:

  • Deciding what the next loop even is. Pointing the agent at the right problem is most of the job. A great agent on the wrong problem looks like progress and isn't.
  • Reading the result with skepticism. Agents over-claim. The reflex of "I don't believe you — show me" is the entire delta between a 12-minute fix and a 12-hour regression.
  • Negotiating taste. Which of three perfectly working approaches is the one the codebase wants. Models do not have a long memory of your codebase's mood. You do.

In aggregate the human role is migrating from producer to reviewer-with-direction. Reviewing is not less work. It's different work, and it gets harder, not easier, as the loop count climbs.

A short prediction

The next year of agent dev will be defined less by who has the smartest model and more by who has the fastest, least frustrating loop end-to-end[2]. The winners will look obvious in retrospect: they reduced the worst-case time-to-verify, they kept review bandwidth as a first-class budget, and they shipped tools that show you a result instead of asking you to imagine one.

I keep a sticky note above my monitor with one line on it: shorter loops, fewer of them. That's the entire job description, restated. Everything else — model choice, framework, agent harness, tool catalogue — is downstream of that sentence.

I'd love to be wrong about some of this. Mail me at matt@pineappleworkshop.com if you are running a loop I should know about.

footnotes

  1. There's a classic Jeff Bezos / Jensen Huang argument that decision velocity beats decision quality past a certain threshold. I think the same is true of loops. A 60% useful loop that closes in fifteen seconds beats a 95% useful loop that closes in fifteen minutes — because the fast loop runs ten times and converges on the right answer before the slow loop finishes once. ↩
  2. The cynical read is that I am writing this because my own loops are still too slow and I want it to be a problem someone else cares about. Both things can be true. ↩
